quality.rationale: 基于四动量不变量在实验室系与质心系间转换，结合高能近似与实际加速器物理背景，逻辑链条清晰且步骤完整。

origin.question: Problem 2.6. Evaluate the "available energy" of two different proton-proton scattering experiments. The first is of the conventional type, where a beam of protons is accelerated to $30\mathrm{GeV}$ and allowed to strike a target (liquid hydrogen, for example). In the second, two separate beams of protons are accelerated to $15\mathrm{GeV}$ each, then directed toward each other and allowed to collide. Evaluate the total energy of two colliding protons in the center of momentum frame for each experiment. To what energy would a beam in the first type of experiment have to be accelerated to match the CM energy of the $15\mathrm{GeV}$ protons in the second experiment?
origin.answer: Fixed-target: $W \approx 7.5\mathrm{GeV}$; Collider: $W = 30\mathrm{GeV}$; Required beam energy for fixed-target: $E \approx 480\mathrm{GeV}$.
origin.solution: Solution 2.6. Let $\mathbf{P}$ and $\mathbf{Q}$ be the 4-momenta of the incident protons, and $\mathbf{W}$ the total energy in the C.M. frame. In this frame,$$\mathbf {W}^{2} = \left(\mathbf {P}^{0} + \mathbf {Q}^{0}\right)^{2} = - \left(\mathbf {P} + \mathbf {Q}\right)^{2},$$which is an invariant expression for $\mathbb{W}^2$ and can be evaluated in any frame. In the conventional experiment, in the lab frame$$\mathbf {P} = (\mathbf {E}, \underline {{\mathbf {P}}})$$$$\mathbf {Q} = \left(m, \underline {{0}}\right),$$so$$\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {W}^{2} = (\mathbf {E} + \mathbf {m})^{2} - \mathbf {p}^{2} \\\\= 2 \mathrm {E m} + 2 \mathrm {m}^{2} \\\\\approx 2 \mathrm {E m} \quad \text {f o r} \quad \mathrm {E} \gg \mathrm {m}. \\\\ \end{array}$$For $\mathbf{E} = 30\mathrm{GeV}$ , $\mathfrak{m} = .94\mathrm{GeV}$ , the available energy is $\mathbf{W} \approx 7.5\mathrm{GeV}$ . For the colliding beam experiment, in the lab frame$$\mathbf {P} = (\mathbf {E}, \underline {{\mathbf {P}}})$$$$\mathbf {Q} = \left(\mathbf {E}, - \underline {{\mathbf {P}}}\right) .$$So $W^2 = 4E^2$ , i.e. $W = 2E$ . For $E = 15 \, \text{GeV}$ , the available energy is $W = 30 \, \text{GeV}$ . To achieve $W = 30 \, \text{GeV}$ in a conventional experiment would require $E = W^2 / 2m \approx 480 \, \text{GeV}$ .
